html:
III

Two days later, in the afternoon, when Jenny was painting in her studio, HelgeвАЩs father called. As he stood with his hat in his hand, she saw that his hair was greyвБ†вАФso grey that she could not make out what the original colour had been, but he still looked young. He was thin, and had a slight stoopвБ†вАФnot the stoop of an old man, but rather of one too slender for his height. His eyes too were young, though sad and tired and so big and blue that they gave one a curious impression of being wide open, surprised, and at the same time suspicious.

вАЬI was very anxious to meet you, Jenny Winge,вАЭ he said, вАЬas you can understand for yourself. No; donвАЩt take off your overall, and tell me if I disturb you.вАЭ

вАЬNot in the least,вАЭ said Jenny warmly. She liked his smile and his voice. She threw her overall on a chair: вАЬThe light is almost gone already. It was very good of you to come and see me.вАЭ

вАЬIt is a very long time since I was in a studio,вАЭ said Gram, sitting down on the sofa.

вАЬDonвАЩt you ever see any of the other paintersвБ†вАФyour contemporaries?вАЭ asked Jenny.

вАЬNo, never,вАЭ he answered curtly.

вАЬButвАЭвБ†вАФJenny bethought herselfвБ†вАФвАЬhow did you find your way up here? Did you ask them at home for my address, or at the artistsвАЩ club?вАЭ

Gram laughed.

вАЬNo; I met you on the stairs the other day, and yesterday, as I was going to the office, I saw you again. I followed you. I was half a mind to stop you and introduce myself. Then I saw you go in here, and I knew there were studios in this house, so I thought I would pay you a visit.вАЭ

вАЬDo you know,вАЭ said Jenny, with a merry laugh, вАЬHelge too followed me in the streetвБ†вАФI was with a friend. He had lost his way in the old streets by the rag market, and he came and spoke to us. That is how we made his acquaintance. We thought it rather cool at the time, but it seems to run in the family.вАЭ

Gram frowned, and sat quiet an instant. Jenny realized that she had said the wrong thing, and was thinking what to say next.

вАЬMay I make you some tea?вАЭ she asked, and without waiting for an answer lit the spirit-lamp under the kettle.

вАЬMiss Winge, you must not be afraid that Helge is like me in other things. I donвАЩt think he takes after his father in anythingвБ†вАФfortunately.вАЭ He laughed. Jenny did not know what to say to this, and busied herself with the tea.

вАЬItвАЩs rather bare in here, as you see, but I live at home with my mother.вАЭ

вАЬI see. This is a good studio, is it not?вАЭ

вАЬI think so.вАЭ

After a moment he said: вАЬI have been thinking of you very much lately, Miss WingeвБ†вАФI understood from my sonвАЩs letters that you and he.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬYes, Helge and I are very fond of each other,вАЭ said Jenny, looking straight at him. He took her hand and held it an instant.

вАЬI know my son so littleвБ†вАФhis real self is almost unknown to me, but as you are fond of him you must know him far better. I have always believed that he was a good boy, and clever in a way, and the fact that you love him proves to me that I have reason to be pleasedвБ†вАФand proud of him. Now that I know you, I can understand that he loves you, and I hope he will make you happy.вАЭ

вАЬThank you,вАЭ said Jenny, giving him her hand again.

вАЬI am fond of the boyвБ†вАФheвАЩs my only sonвБ†вАФand I think he likes me too.вАЭ

вАЬI know he does. Helge is very fond of you and of his mother.вАЭ She blushed as if she had been tactless.

вАЬYes, I believe so; but he must have seen long ago that his father and mother did not care for one another. Helge has not had a happy home, Jenny. I donвАЩt mind telling you this, for if you have not already understood it, you will soon see it for yourself. You are a sensible girl. HelgeвАЩs experience of his own home will teach him, perhaps, to value your love and try to keep it.вАЭ

Jenny poured out the tea: вАЬHelge used to come and have tea with me in the afternoon in RomeвБ†вАФit was really during these visits we learnt to know each other, I think.вАЭ

вАЬAnd you became fond of each other?вАЭ

вАЬNo, not at once. Perhaps we were, thoughвБ†вАФeven thenвБ†вАФbut we believed that we were great friends only. He came to tea afterwards too, of course.вАЭ They both smiled.

вАЬTell me something about Helge from the time he was a boyвБ†вАФwhen he was quite small, I mean.вАЭ

Gram smiled sadly and shook his head: вАЬNo; I cannot tell you anything about my son. He was always good and obedient, and did well at school. He was not particularly clever, but he worked steadily and diligently. He was very reserved as a boyвБ†вАФand later, too, for that matterвБ†вАФwith me, anyhow. You, I am sure, have more to tell me.вАЭ

вАЬAbout what?вАЭ

вАЬAbout Helge, of course. Tell me what he looks like to the girl who loves him. You are no ordinary girl eitherвБ†вАФyou are an artistвБ†вАФand I believe you are intelligent and good. Will you not tell me how you came to like himвБ†вАФwhat it was that made you choose him?вАЭ

вАЬWell,вАЭ she said laughinglyвБ†вАФвАЬit is not so easy to sayвБ†вАФwe just got fond of each other.вАЭ

He laughed too. вАЬWell, it was a stupid question, I admit. One would say I had quite forgotten what it was to be young and in love, donвАЩt you think?вАЭ

вАЬDonвАЩt you think!вБ†вАФHelge says that so often, too. It was one of the things that made me like him. He was so young. I saw that he was very reserved, but gradually thawed a good deal.вАЭ

вАЬI can understand he wouldвБ†вАФto you. Tell me more! Oh, but donвАЩt look so frightened. I donвАЩt mean that you should tell me the whole story. Only tell me something about yourself and about Helge, about your workвБ†вАФand about Rome. I am an old man. I want to feel again what it is like to be an artistвБ†вАФand free. To work at the only thing you care forвБ†вАФto be youngвБ†вАФand in loveвБ†вАФand happy.вАЭ

He stayed for two hours. When he was ready to go and stood with his hat in his hand, he said in a low voice: вАЬIt is no use trying to hide from you the state of things at home. When we meet there, it would be better if we pretended not to have met before. I donвАЩt wish HelgeвАЩs mother to know that I have made your acquaintance in this wayвБ†вАФfor your sake, so as not to expose you to any disagreeable, malicious words from her. It is enough for her to know that I like somebodyвБ†вАФespecially if it is a womanвБ†вАФto turn her against them. You think it strange, I am sure, but you understand, donвАЩt you?вАЭ

вАЬYes,вАЭ said Jenny quietly.

вАЬGoodbye. I am happy about you for HelgeвАЩs sakeвБ†вАФbelieve me, Jenny.вАЭ

She had written to Helge the night before about her visit to his home, and when she read her letter through, she realized how very cold and poor was the part about her meeting with his mother. When writing to him that night she told him about his fatherвАЩs visit, but she tore the letter up and began another. It was so difficult to tell him about his fatherвАЩs call and not to mention hers to Mrs.¬†Gram. She did not like having secrets with one from the other. She felt humiliated on HelgeвАЩs behalf at having been initiated all at once in the misery of his home, and she ended by not saying a word about it in her letterвБ†вАФit would be easier to explain when he came.
